coredata.dc:title: A highly-specific photoelectrochemical platform based on carbon nanodots and polymers functionalized organic-inorganic perovskite for cholesterol sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lu
Song, Minxia
Guan, Jie
Shu, Yun
Jin, Dangqin
Fan, Gaochao
Xu, Qin
Hu, Xiao-Ya
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we reported the introduction of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as additives into perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 through in situ synthesis to prepare PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs composite, which demonstrated improved water tolerance and mechanical stability. The application of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs for photoelectrochemical sensing was then explored. A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) that could specifically recognize cholesterol (CHO) was anchored to PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs via a simple thermal polymerization process, followed by elution with hexane. A label-free and sensitive photoelectrochemical method for CHO detection was achieved by using the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs platform. The detection limit for CHO was 2.1 × 10−14 mol/L, lower than most of the existing CHO detection methods. In our perception, this platform can be extended to numerous other analytes. This research result may provide a new understanding to improve the performance and broaden the application range of organic-inorganic perovskites.

Cholesterol

SEM images of and the contact angle measurements (insets of Figures) of (A) CH3NH3PbI3/ITO, (B) PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3/ITO, (C) PVDF- CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, (D) MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, and (E) NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO.

(A) The I-t responses of CH3NH3PbI3/ITO (a) PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3/ITO (b) and PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (c) in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. (B) XRD spectra of the CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a), PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (curve b), PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c), MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve d) and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve e). (C) Steady-state PL spectra of CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a), PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (curve b) and PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c). (D) IMVS of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve a) and MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve b).

(A) The photocurrent responses of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (a) and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (b) after removal of CHO, NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (c) and MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (d) after incubation in 1.0 × 10−9 moL/L CHO. (C) The linear calibration curves for MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor. (D) Selectivity of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO on CHO and the interference substances. The concentration of CHO was 0.10 nM and the interferences were 1.0 nM.

head.ce:title: A highly-specific photoelectrochemical platform based on carbon nanodots and polymers functionalized organic-inorganic perovskite for cholesterol sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we reported the introduction of carbon nanodots (CNDs) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as additives into perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 through in situ synthesis to prepare PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs composite, which demonstrated improved water tolerance and mechanical stability. The application of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs for photoelectrochemical sensing was then explored. A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) that could specifically recognize cholesterol (CHO) was anchored to PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs via a simple thermal polymerization process, followed by elution with hexane. A label-free and sensitive photoelectrochemical method for CHO detection was achieved by using the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs platform. The detection limit for CHO was 2.1 × 10−14 mol/L, lower than most of the existing CHO detection methods. In our perception, this platform can be extended to numerous other analytes. This research result may provide a new understanding to improve the performance and broaden the application range of organic-inorganic perovskites.

ce:section.ce:para: Enormous progress has been achieved recently in photoelectrochemical sensors (PEC), which separate the excitation light source and photocurrent detection of electrochemical signals. PECs have advantages that include minimal background signal, sensitive detection, low cost, easy operation, and on-site analysis [1]. Photoactive materials such as quantum dots [2], semiconductors [3], conducting polymers [4] et al. are important components of PEC sensors. However, the implementation of these materials in PEC sensors is challenged by various problems such as complex preparation process, low and unstable photocurrents. Therefore, discovery of new photoactive materials is critical for the development of PEC sensors.

Hybrid organic-inorganic perovskites (OIPs) are outstanding photoactive candidates because of their facile preparation process, long charge carrier lifetime and diffusion length, high absorption coefficient and other excellent properties [5]. OIPs have been widely studied in the areas of solar cells, light emitting diode and other optoelectronic devices [6]. Despite their superior photovoltaic properties, the instability of OIPs in the presence of oxygen and water is the main impediment that hindered their applications in PEC sensors [7]. Fast degradation of OIPs happens when they are exposed to moisture, not to mention in aqueous solutions. Considerable attention has been devoted to improve the stability of OIPs, such as encapsulation using polymers [8] or silica [9], and surface modification with water-proof ligands [10]. Polymer encapsulation was proved to be the simplest and has the highest efficiency; however this method could only improve the stability of OIPs to a certain degree. The encapsulation also restricts the charge transport of OIPs, therefore limits its further photoelectrochemical applications. Thus it is imperative to develop new strategies to prepare water-stable OIPs [11].

Additives such as nanoparticles [12] and halide salts [13] have been proved to enhance the charge transfer properties of OIPs. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have grasped the attention of numerous research groups because of their low toxicity, high chemical stability, better biocompatibility and water resistance [14,15]. Furthermore, the functional carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CNDs could passivate the uncoordinated lead ions and form hydrogen bonds with halide ions [15]. CNDs also have been demonstrated to improve the performance of OIPs based devices [16]. In most cases, an ultrathin CNDs layer or a graphene quantum dot layer is inserted between the perovskite and TiO2 [17]. By adding a CND layer, the electron mobility and the electron extraction ability of the electron transport layer can be greatly improved. CNDs have also been used as sensitizers for all-inorganic CsPbBr3 inverse opal perovskite solar cells, which can enhance light absorption and facilitate charge transfer. However, most of the studies focused on the application of CNDs as the hole-transport layer in OIPs based solar cells [18,19]. There are few studies on the application of CNDs in improving the water stability of OIPs, especially in the PEC sensor construction area.

Herein, CNDs addition and polymers encapsulation were combined to improve the water stability and charge transfer properties of CH3NH3PbI3. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), a hydrophobic polymer with superior piezoelectric, mechanical and film forming properties, was selected to be the polymer matrix for CH3NH3PbI3 encapsulation [20]. In situ growth of CH3NH3PbI3 in a polymer matrix containing CNDs had been developed. This PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs showed much higher stability than pristine CH3NH3PbI3, which could have extensive applications in different areas.

The formed PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs was used as the photoactive layer for the construction of a PEC sensor. Cholesterol (CHO) is a type of lipid which can help maintain the structural integrity and fluidity of cell membranes [21]. However, excessive CHO in the serum may impede blood circulation, causing various cardiovascular diseases [22]. Detection of CHO is very important in medical labs; thus it was chosen as the target molecule in this research to test the performance of a OIPs based PEC platform. Various methods of detecting cholesterol have been reported, such as high performance liquid chromatography [23], gas chromatography [24], fluorescence method [25], etc. However, most of these methods required expensive and complicated instruments. The electrochemical methods are simple but lack selectivity. Cholesterol esterase or cholesterol oxidase were always used to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of the electrochemical sensors [26]. As an important biological receptor, the enzyme can only be used in a suitable temperature or pH range, with limited application range and low sensitivity [27].

Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) have been welcomed by researchers because of their desirable selectivity and chemical stability [28,29]. MIPs, in combination with PEC technique, offers the promise of highly selective and sensitive detection of monitoring targets with low cost [30]. In this research, a simple thermal polymerization process was applied to imprint CHO on the photoactive PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs composite by using CHO as the template, methacrylic acid (MAA) and styrene (St) as the functional monomers, ethylene glycol dimethacylate (EGDMA) as the cross-linker, and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) as the initiator, respectively. Scheme 1 illustrated molecular imprinting and the PEC platform construction process. After the imprinted CHO template was removed, specific imprinting sites of the CHO template appeared on the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO electrode surface. During the CHO detection process, the recognition of CHO was achieved by the unique shape of CHO molecule on the MIPs film, van der Waals’ and hydrogen-bonding interactions. As expected, the selective entrapment of CHO on the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO induced the steric hindrances towards the transfer of electrons, thus leading to the decrease of the photocurrent. The quantification of CHO was based on the change of the photoelectrochemical currents [31,32]. The practicality of this PEC sensor was demonstrated by using it to determine the CHO concentrations in human serums.

All chemicals were used without further purification. The indium tin oxide coated glasses (ITO, 8 Ω/square) were purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd (Zhuhai, China). PbI2 (99%), EGDMA, PVDF (average molecular weight: 400000), acetaminophen (AP), arachidonic acid (AC) and glyceryl tridodecanoate (GT) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Cooperation (Shanghai. China). Methylammonium iodide (MAI, 98%), CHO (99%) were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). MAA, AIBN, St, DMF, hexane, ethyl acetate, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, toluene, acetone and ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Uric acid (UA) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. The serum samples were obtained from Subei People's Hospital.

PEC and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) signals were obtained from CIMPS-02 workstation (Zennium, Zahner-ElektrikGmbH & CoKG, Germany) by using a white light source (500 W, WLC02) ((4300 k) # 1522, Zahner-Elektrik) irradiation. A three-electrode system was used in this work by using ITOs modified by different materials as the working electrodes, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and a Pt cylinder as the auxiliary electrode. The ITO substrates were subsequently cleaned with toluene, acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for 20 min, respectively, and then dried by N2. The SEM images were obtained from a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Zeiss Supra 55, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were employed on JEM-2100 (Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100 keV. Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Model: TENSOR27, Germany) was used to obtained the FT-IR spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured by using an XRD-7000 (Shimadzu, Japan) diffractometer with a Cu target radiation resource (λ = 1.5406 Å). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed on Spectrofluorometer FS5 (Edinburgh, England). The contact angle results were recorded by OCA20 (Dataphysics, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America).

CNDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal treatment [33] except that grape juice was used as the carbon source in this work. In a typical procedure, 30 mL of grape juice was transferred into an 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was heated at a constant temperature of 180 °C for 8 h. After the hydrothermal reaction was completed, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove the large particles. Excess acetone was added to the upper brown solution and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the CNDs.

CH3NH3PbI3 precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 0.9220 g PbI2 and 0.3179 g MAI in 2 mL DMF, then the solution was heated under vigorously stirring at 70 °C for 6 h. The precursor solution of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs was prepared using one-step method in the light of previous reports with some modifications [34]. Firstly, PVDF was dissolved in DMF to give a concentration of 20 mg/mL. Then 0.9220 g PbI2, 0.3179 g MAI and 20 mg CNDs were dispersed and dissolved in 2 mL PVDF solution. The mixture was heated and stirred in a water bath at 70 °C for 6 h before use. The formed solution was black with a slightly viscous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MIPs and non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) precursor solution
ce:section.ce:para: The MIPs precursor solution was prepared as follows: 0.3867 g (1 mmoL) of CHO, 326 μL of MAA and 145 μL of St (total 5 mmoL) as functional monomers were dissolved in 10.0 mL of DMF. Then, the solution was shaken at 35 °C for 6 h. Next, 500 μL (2.7 mmol) EGDMA and 6.5 mg (40 μmol) AIBN were added to the solution, followed by drying with nitrogen for 30 min at room temperature to obtain the MIPs precursor solution. The NIPs precursor solution was prepared in the same way without the presence of CHO.

The previously prepared MIPs precursors (225 μL) were mixed with 2 mL of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs precursor solution. Then the mixture was shaken violently for 5 min before 15 μL of the mixed solution was casted on the ITO with an active surface area of 1 cm2. The MIP@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs precursor solution deposited substrates were dried at 60 °C for 6 h in an oven. As a control, the NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs PEC sensor was prepared in the same way except the mixture of the NIPs precursor solution with PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs precursor solution was deposited on ITO. The templates molecules were eluted with hexane for 15 min.

All the photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution at the bias potential of 0.0 V (versus Ag/AgCl) under the irradiation of visible light. For CHO detection, the original photocurrent of MIPs@PVDF- CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 was denoted as I0. Then, the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO electrode was immersed in ethyl acetate which contained different concentrations of CHO with constant stirring at the speed of 750 rpm. After 15 min, the electrode was taken out and measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution again. The current measured after the recognition of CHO was recorded as Is. Based on the difference between Is and I0 (ΔI = I0-Is), the concentration of CHO was calculated. For serum sample analysis, the samples were serially diluted by ethyl acetate. These dilutions were prepared by adding 100 μL of the serum samples to 1.0 ml of ethyl acetate and performing six tenfold serial dilutions with ethyl acetate as the diluent. Recovery of CHO was determined by using the diluted serum as blank serum matrix that was spiked with CHO standard. The recovery was expressed as the percentage of the added CHO.

The structure of the as-prepared CNDs used in this work was shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM image showed the diameter of CNDs was about 20–30 nm (Fig. 1A). Fig. 1B showed the FTIR spectrum of CNDs. The broad peaks in the range of 3258 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [35]. The stretching vibrations of C–H (2923 cm−1), CO (1702 cm−1) and CC (1613 cm−1) were observed, demonstrating the presence of alkyl functional groups on CNDs [35,36]. The carboxylic groups, hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the edge of CNDs would form strong and stable interactions with the uncoordinated Pb in MAPbI3. This process would stabilize MAPbI3 via passivating the grain boundaries and improve the stability of CH3NH3PbI3 [36]. Fig. 1C exhibited the XPS survey spectrum of CNDs. The binding energy peaks around 284.7, 400.7 and 534.4 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [37]. Calculations indicated the CNDs were composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen with atomic percentages of 75.75%, 22.19%, and 2.06%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum was shown in Fig. 1D. The peaks at 284.5, 285.7 and 287.3 eV could be assigned to groups of C–C, C–N, CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1E) corresponding to three peaks could be identified at 531.1, 532.6 and 534.1 eV, indicating the chemical environments for C–OH/C–O–C, CO and H–O–H, respectively [33]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) showed four peaks at 398.2, 399.1, 400.2 and 402.6 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic and oxidized nitrogen, respectively [38,39]. The presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen is beneficial to the interaction between CNDs with uncoordinated lead cations [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CH3NH3PbI3 nanocomposites and PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CNDs and polymers on the morphologies of CH3NH3PbI3 were studied by SEM. Fig. 2 showed the SEM images of CH3NH3PbI3, PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3, PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs films. All the films were comprised of dense grains. Distinct differences were observed in the grain sizes and the height of the grain boundaries. For the pristine CH3NH3PbI3 film (Fig. 2A-1~2), the grain size ranged from 2 to 5 μm. Big grain boundaries were observed. These grain boundaries with dangling bonds can act as charge carrier trap centers and cause non-radiative recombination to degrade the photophysical properties of the perovskite film and further to trigger the degradation of the materials and its physical properties [40]. Fig. 2B-1~2 showed that the grain boundaries were reduced in the presence of PVDF. PVDF helps to control the crystallization rate of the perovskite [41]. It bridges the perovskite grains, minimizing grain-to-grain electrical decoupling and yielding excellent environmental stability against moisture, light, and heat, which has not been attainable with small molecule defect passivating agents. When both CNDs and PVDF were introduced into CH3NH3PbI3, the grain boundaries of the obtained CH3NH3PbI3 were significantly reduced and the grain size became smaller than PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (Fig. 2C-1~2). This demonstrated the synergistic effect of CNDs and PVDF on the formation of perovskite. The dangling –OH, –CO, and –COOH bonds on CNDs can form strong bonds with perovskite and form massive heterogeneous nucleation sites, which influence the crystal growth [42]. Therefore, more crystals with small size were formed. The effects of CNDs amount on the morphology and stability of CH3NH3PbI3 were discussed in the supporting information (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, Supporting information). Fig. 2D-1~2 and E-1~2 showed the SEM images of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs. Distinct differences were noticed between the two samples. The MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (without the elution of CHO) film showed significant difference when compared with the other films. The formed MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs film was porous (Fig. 2D-1). It was composed of small nanoparticles with blurred boundaries (Fig. 2D-2). This morphology could facilitate mass transfer and improve the recognition efficiency [43]. CHO, a rigid molecular with hydrophobic characters, would affect the morphology of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs. In the absence of CHO, the morphology of NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (Fig. 2E) was similar to that of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs. The differences of Fig. 2D and E also indicated the effect of CHO in the formation of perovskite film. It was imprinted into the film by the thermal polymerization process.

The contact angles were measured to characterize the water resistance of different perovskite films. Compared with CH3NH3PbI3 with a contact angle of 23.8° (Fig. 2A-2 inset), the contact angle of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (Fig. 2B-2 inset) increased to 41.4°. When CNDs and PVDF were introduced into the preparation of CH3NH3PbI3, the contact angle of the formed film was increased to 50.8° (Fig. 2C-2 inset). This was because of the synergistic effect of CNDs and PVDF on the water resistance of the obtained film. When CHO was introduced into the perovskite precursor solution, the contact angle of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (Fig. 2D-2 inset) was increased to 75.9°. However, for NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, the contact angle was 60.2° (Fig. 2E-2 inset). The contact angle difference between MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs further indicated the successful incorporation of CHO by the imprinting process. CHO is a hydrophobic lipid substance. Its presence will improve the water resistance of the obtained film.

The effects of CNDs and PVDF on the water stability of CH3NH3PbI3 were further studied by checking their photocurrents in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution under the illumination of visible light. Fig. 3 A showed that the photocurrents of the pristine CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a) and PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (curve b) decreased to 23% and 34% of their initial values in 420s, respectively. But PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c) retained 97% of its initial value. The results suggested superior aqueous stability of the PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs film. CNDs and PVDF can greatly protect perovskite film from decomposition under moisture attack and significantly improve the stability of PEC in very humid environments. The roles of CNDs and PVDF can be summarized as follows: First, CNDs can reduce the perovskite grain boundaries; while the simultaneously formed PVDF scaffold, which is formed by entangled long-chain molecules, acts as a three-dimensional skeleton to support perovskite crystals, enabling it to cover the substrate more uniformly and thus demonstrating an advantage over CNDs only. Secondly, the wetting properties of the precursor solution have been improved after the introduction of PVDF and CNDs, which can passivate grain boundary. Thirdly, PVDF molecules in the precursor solution can slow down the crystallization of perovskite by influencing the intermediate phase in the crystallization process.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 3B unfolded the crystalline information of CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a), PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (curve b), PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c), MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve d), and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve e). All of the samples exhibited strong diffraction peaks at 14.31°, 28.98°, 32.56° and 41.15°, corresponding to (110), (220), (310) and (224) crystal facets, respectively. These signals demonstrated the tetragonal structural characteristics of CH3NH3PbI3 [18,44]. There was no significant change in peak positions in the presence of different additives (curve a-e). Therefore, the crystal structure of CH3NH3PbI3 was not affected when PVDF, CNDs, MIPs or NIPs were presented in the precursor solution.

PL emission spectroscopies can be used to evaluate the ability of photo-introduced carrier efficiency and charge separation behavior in semiconductor materials [45]. As shown in Fig. 3C, compared with CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a), the PL intensity of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3 (curve b) were effectively enhanced. The most intensive peak was observed in PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c). The PL results also indicated the synergetic effect of CNDs and PVDF. CNDs and PVDF have lone pair electrons which could coordinate with defect sites at grain boundaries. Higher PL intensity is an indicator of more efficient photoexciton and slower hole-electron recombination [15], so the presence of CNDs and PVDF plays positive roles on the passivation effect of charge-carrier trapping states. Furthermore, the emission peak position of CH3NH3PbI3 at 791 nm moved to 785 nm when PVDF and CNDs were presented. The blue shift indicated the attachment of PVDF and CNDs onto CH3NH3PbI3, which changed the dielectric constant of the medium and the size of CH3NH3PbI3 [7].

IMVS can be used to investigate charge recombination and electron lifetime of CH3NH3PbI3 prepared in the presence of CNDs and PVDF [46]. Fig. 3D showed the IMVS responses of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve a) and MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve b) measured at the open circuit voltage. Both of them showed semicircular in the Nyquist plot. The minimum frequency value in the Nyquist plot is related to the charge recombination time constant [47]. The effective electron lifetime (tr) can be derived by the formula tr = 1/2π fmin in which fmin was the minimum characteristic frequency of the IMVS imaginary component [48]. The electron lifetime of PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO and MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO were 172.63 ms and 292.84 ms, respectively. The electron lifetime significantly increased when MIPs was added into PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs solution, indicating the recombination of electron-hole pairs was effectively suppressed. This suggested that MIPs increased the diffusion length of electrons, thereby prolonging the movement time of electrons [47].

The feasibility of the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC platform for CHO determination was investigated. Fig. 4 A showed the comparison of the photocurrents of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO before and after the recognition of 1.0 × 10−9 M CHO. For MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve a and curve d), the photocurrent changed from −6.7 μA (curve a) to −2.9 μA (curve d) before and after the recognition of CHO. For NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve b and c), the photocurrent changed from −6.0 μA (curve b) to −5.6 μA (curve c) before and after the recognition of CHO. The photocurrent change of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was about 10 times that of NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, indicating the special recognition ability of the MIPs layer for CHO. CHO occupied the imprinting sites after recognition, impeding the transport of electrons and thus causing the decrease of the photocurrents. The slight current change on NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO may be due to the physical adsorption of CHO on the film.

Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S3, Supporting information), the PEC sensor was used to detect CHO of diverse concentrations. The photocurrent density gradually decreased along with the increased CHO concentrations (Fig. 4B) with a linear range from 1.0 × 10−13 to 5.0 × 10−8 mol/L. The linear regression equation of the PEC sensor was ΔI (μA) = 13.1784 + 0.9619lg C (R2 = 0.9979) (Fig. 4C). Based on the standard deviation of the response and the slope, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.1 × 10−14 mol/L. The quantification limit is 7.13 × 10−14 mol/L based on 10Sa/b [49]. This MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor displayed a wider liner ranger and lower detection limit than previously reported studies (Table 1 ).

To evaluate the selectivity of the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor, common substances in serum were used as interferences, including uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, fatty acid (FA), triglyceride (TG) and acetaminophene (AP). Fig. 4D showed the photocurrent density of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO and NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor after the recognition of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L CHO alone and solutions with a interference. The photocurrent change of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor to CHO was remarkably higher than those of the interferences. However, NIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor showed little differences in the photocurrent changes between CHO and the interferences, indicating high selectivity of MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor.

The practical applicability of the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor was tested by using it to determine the CHO concentration in a serum sample. The serum sample was diluted with ethyl acetate without performing other pretreatment process. The concentration of CHO in the serum sample detected by the PEC sensor was 3.32 × 10−3 mol/L, which was close to that detected by a commercial method (3.48 × 10−3 mol/L). The applicability of the PEC sensor was also checked by using it to measure the recovery of CHO from serum samples. Different amounts of CHO standard were added into a diluted serum samples. The CHO concentrations were then determined by the PEC sensor. Table 2 showed the recoveries of CHO ranged from 98.1% to 105.9% with RSD less than 4.41%. These results indicated the prepared PEC sensor had high credibility for real sample analysis.

The reproducibility of the MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO electrode was investigated with five electrodes which produced under the same conditions. The photochemical responses of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L CHO on these electrodes were checked. A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.87% was obtained for the five electrodes, showing an acceptable reproducibility. The repeatability and mechanical stability of the sensor was estimated by repeating test of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L CHO by the same MIPs@PVDF-CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO electrode. It didn't show any significant changes in the responses even after 10 times elution and recognition process, indicating the good mechanism stability of the sensor. The RSD in ten measurements with the same sensor was 4.37%, indicating acceptable mechanism stability and repeatability.

In this work, we proposed a strategy that combined CNDs doping and polymer encapsulation to provide a stable CH3NH3PbI3 based photoelectrochemical platform for CHO sensing. PVDF was added as a protective layer, which improved the water tolerance. The cheap and readily available CNDs were used to passivate the perovskite grain boundaries by their functional carbonyl groups and hydroxyl groups. These groups can passivate uncoordinated lead ions and form hydrogen bonds with halide ions, thus increase the water stability of perovskite and improve the charge separation of CH3NH3PbI3. The introduction of CNDs and PVDF made the application of CH3NH3PbI3 in aqueous solution possible. Meanwhile, the introduction of MIPs with good selectivity and chemical stability enabled the perovskite PEC sensor to have specific recognition capabilities. Using CHO as a template, its high selectivity has been verified with human serum samples. Because of its simple manufacturing process, low cost, and excellent water tolerance properties, the CNDs and polymers functionalized CH3NH3PbI3 would have broad application prospects in the photoelectrochemical related areas.
